title: Design Thinking Meets AI
subtitle: From "what if?" to a real solution — your project begins

diagram.alt: The Design Thinking wheel — Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test — with AI at the centre

overview: Today we bridge understanding and building. Design thinking is the problem-solving framework used by the world's most innovative companies.
Today you will combine design thinking with your AI knowledge to identify a real problem, design an AI-powered solution, and plan a mini-project you will build over the next two days.
objectives: Apply the 5-stage design thinking process to a real problem
Identify where AI adds genuine value vs where human judgment is essential
Design a feasible AI solution with a defined scope
Create a project plan for Days 8–9
Present a project pitch to the class for peer feedback

concepts.heading: Design Thinking — The 5 Stages
body: Design thinking is a five-stage loop for solving real problems. You move through each stage and often circle back as you learn more:

rows: Understand the people who have the problem. Do not assume — ask, observe, listen. Who is affected? How does this problem feel to them?
Clearly state the problem. Use this format: "[Person] needs [need] because [insight]." Avoid jumping to solutions.
Generate as many ideas as possible without judging any of them. Quantity over quality at this stage. Wild ideas welcome.
Build the simplest possible version of your solution — fast and cheap. A sketch, a flowchart, a simple demo is fine.
Get real feedback. Show your prototype to someone who has the problem. What works? What doesn't? Iterate.

concepts.heading: Where Does AI Actually Help?
body: Not every problem needs AI. Here is a framework for deciding when AI adds value.
bullets: AI is a good fit when the task involves pattern recognition in large amounts of data (e.g., identifying disease in thousands of images).
AI is a good fit when personalisation at scale is needed (e.g., recommending different content to millions of users).
AI is a good fit when speed matters more than occasional errors (e.g., spam filtering — some false positives are acceptable).
AI is a good fit when the task is repetitive and rule-based at its core, but with too many variations for manual rules.
AI is a good fit when natural language understanding or generation is central to the problem.

text: AI is NOT a good fit when the decision has life-or-death consequences and errors are unacceptable without human review.
AI is NOT a good fit when you have very little training data (AI needs lots of examples to work well).
AI is NOT a good fit when the problem requires genuine common sense, novel reasoning, or emotional nuance.
AI is NOT a good fit when transparency and explainability are legally or ethically required (e.g., credit decisions, medical diagnosis).
AI is NOT a good fit when a simple rule-based system or spreadsheet would do the job just as well.

concepts.heading: Project Options for Days 8–9
body: Choose one of the following project tracks. Each is achievable in approximately 2 × 3 hours with the provided tools:

rows: Build a custom AI assistant for a specific topic using a system prompt. Example: a "homework helper" that only assists with science questions, always shows working, and never just gives the answer.
Build an image classifier using Teachable Machine and embed it in a simple webpage. Example: a "plant health checker" that identifies healthy vs diseased leaves.
Build an interactive choose-your-own-adventure story powered by AI. The reader's choices update the AI prompt, generating new story branches.
Design a prompt system that helps students research a topic responsibly — breaking down a topic, suggesting sources, summarising findings, and flagging potential misinformation.

activities.name: Problem Sprint
intro: Brainstorm real problems fast, cluster them with your team, and pick ONE worth solving. Then sharpen it into a clear problem statement.
steps: On your own, write one problem per sticky note — from school, home, your community, your interests. No filtering. (5-minute timer.)
Form a team of 3 and cluster all your problems by theme on a large sheet of paper.
As a team, pick ONE problem to pursue. It should be specific, genuinely felt, and potentially solvable with a digital tool.
Define it using the template — WHO has this problem? (Be specific — "Year 7 students who study at home alone" not just "students".)
WHAT is the problem? (Describe the situation, not the solution.)
WHY does it matter? What is the impact if it remains unsolved?
HOW do people currently solve it? What is wrong with the current solution?

Write your Problem Statement: "[Person] needs [need] because [insight]." Example: "Year 7 students studying alone at home need a way to check their own understanding of maths concepts because they have no one to ask and get stuck for hours, losing motivation."
facilitator: Give each student 10 sticky notes. Set a 5-minute timer.
Task: Write one problem per sticky note — problems from school, home, your community, your interests. No filtering.
Group students into teams of 3. Each team clusters their problems by theme on a large sheet of paper.
Teams pick ONE problem to pursue. It should be: specific, genuinely felt, and potentially solvable with a digital tool.

activities.name: AI Fit Canvas
intro: As a team, complete the Project Canvas. It walks you from the problem, to the AI solution, to a build plan, and finally to the risks and ethics you'll need to manage.
steps: Section 1 — The Problem: Write your Problem Statement (from Activity 1). Who is our primary user? What emotion does this problem cause (frustration, confusion, fear, loneliness)?

Section 2 — The AI Solution: What will our AI do? (1 sentence). What type of AI is it (chatbot / image classifier / generator / other)? What training data or prompts does it need? What does the AI decide, and what does the human decide?
Section 3 — Build Plan: Which Project Track (A / B / C / D)? Tools we will use? Day 8 goal? Day 9 goal? How will we know if our solution works (success measure)?
Section 4 — Risks & Ethics: What could go wrong? Which ethical principle (fairness / privacy / transparency / safety) is most relevant, and how will we address it?
facilitator: Hand out the Project Canvas worksheet — teams complete it together.
Circulate and push teams to be specific about what the AI decides versus what the human decides.
Close with 60-second project pitches per team, then confirm team formation and lock in project plans.

reflection: What problem did your team choose? Why does it matter to you personally?
Where in your solution does AI add value that a human or simple app could not? Be specific.
What is the one thing that could most easily go wrong with your project? How will you handle it?
Describe your ideal user testing session: Who would you show your prototype to? What question would you most want them to answer?

quiz.q: What is the correct order of the 5 stages of design thinking?
quiz.explain: The five stages run Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test — understand the people first, state the problem clearly, brainstorm widely, build a simple version, then test it with real feedback.

quiz.q: During the Ideate stage, what is the main rule?
options: Pick the single best idea immediately
Only suggest ideas that are guaranteed to work
Generate as many ideas as possible without judging any of them
Build a working prototype before sharing any idea
quiz.explain: Ideate is about quantity over quality: generate as many ideas as possible, welcome wild ones, and hold off on judging. The filtering happens later.

quiz.q: A good problem definition should already include the solution you plan to build.
quiz.explain: False. The Define stage describes the situation, not the solution — "[Person] needs [need] because [insight]." Jumping to a solution too early locks you out of better ideas.

quiz.q: Which of these is the BEST fit for an AI solution?
options: Spotting patterns across thousands of medical images
A life-or-death decision where any error is unacceptable and there is no human review
A task where you only have five examples to learn from
A job a simple spreadsheet could already do perfectly
quiz.explain: AI shines at pattern recognition in large amounts of data. The other options are exactly the cases where AI is a poor fit — high-stakes errors, too little data, or a simpler tool would do.

quiz.q: If transparency and explainability are legally required (like credit or medical decisions), a "black box" AI is often a poor fit.
quiz.explain: True. When the law or ethics demand that you explain a decision, an AI you can't explain is a poor fit. That is one of the key situations where AI is NOT a good choice.

rows: Day 6 gallery walk review — favourite creative pieces
Key Concepts: Design Thinking + AI use case mapping
Activity 1: Problem Sprint — identify and define a problem
Activity 2: Solution Design + AI Fit Canvas
Team formation confirmed, project plans locked in
